title: Tanacetum × marginatum
intro: Tanacetum × marginatum, also known as Tansy, is a perennial herb belonging to the sunflower family (Asteraceae) and is a hybrid of T. vulgare and T. coccineum. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its health benefits, which include its potential to aid in digestion, relieve menstrual cramps, and exhibit antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents of Tanacetum × marginatum include sesquiterpene lactones, particularly tanacetin and camphor, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Tansy is commonly used to make herbal teas, tinctures, and infusions, as well as being incorporated into salves and creams for topical applications.
taxonomy: Tanacetum × marginatum is classified as a hybrid of the Tanacetum genus, specifically a cross between Tanacetum balsamita and Tanacetum coccineum. It belongs to the Asteraceae family, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This classification places it within the larger group of flowering plants known as angiosperms. The Tanacetum × marginatum is further classified as a perennial herb, with properties and characteristics unique to its specific hybrid status. Its classification is an essential aspect of understanding its growth, behavior, and potential uses in various fields.
uses: Tanacetum × marginatum, also known as margined tansy, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant's leaves and flowers are used to treat fever, rheumatism, and other inflammatory conditions. It is also believed to have antiseptic and antibacterial properties, which can help to heal wounds and prevent infection. Additionally, Tanacetum × marginatum has been used to treat digestive issues such as indigestion and diarrhea. However, its medicinal uses should be further researched and confirmed for their effectiveness and safety.

Its essential oil has been studied for its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which could be beneficial in wound healing and skin care. Tanacetum × marginatum has also been traditionally used to treat fever, rheumatism, and respiratory problems, such as bronchitis and asthma. constituents: Tanacetum × marginatum contains sesquiterpene lactones, particularly parthenolide, which is responsible for its medicinal properties. These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antiproliferative effects. The parthenolide content in Tanacetum × marginatum has been studied for its potential in cancer treatment and its ability to inhibit the growth of cancer cells. Additionally, the plant's sesquiterpene lactones have been found to have antimicrobial and antifungal properties. Further research is needed to fully understand the active constituents and their effects on human health.

side_effects: Tanacetum × marginatum, commonly known as White Tansy or Margined Tansy, is not well-documented for its use as a medicinal herb, but its relatives in the Tanacetum genus have been known to have potential side effects. Some members of the Tanacetum genus may cause skin irritation, allergic reactions, or interact with medications in certain individuals.
